meaningEn: to remember or consider something
examples: Keep in mind that the deadline is Friday.
Please keep in mind that users may have slow internet.
Keep in mind that clear communication saves time.

conversation.question: Should we release it today?
conversation.answer: Yes, but keep in mind that QA has not tested it yet.

meaningEn: to think of an idea or plan
examples: Can you come up with a better title?
The team came up with a simple solution.
I need to come up with examples for my presentation.

conversation.question: Do we have any ideas for the homepage?
conversation.answer: Not yet, but I'll come up with a few options today.

meaningEn: to be clear, logical, or easy to understand
examples: Your explanation makes sense.
This folder structure makes sense for a small app.
It makes sense to practice speaking every day.

conversation.question: Should we split this component?
conversation.answer: Yes, it makes sense because it is getting too large.

meaningEn: to handle or manage a problem or situation
examples: I need to deal with some emails first.
The app should deal with empty data safely.
Good leaders know how to deal with pressure.

conversation.question: Can you fix the login issue?
conversation.answer: Yes, I'll deal with it after the standup.

meaningEn: to mention or show something important
examples: She pointed out a mistake in my report.
The reviewer pointed out a missing test case.
I want to point out one risk before we continue.

conversation.question: Did anyone notice the bug?
conversation.answer: Yes, Huy pointed it out during review.

meaningEn: to consider something when making a decision
examples: We should take cost into account.
The design should take accessibility into account.
When planning your career, take your strengths into account.

conversation.question: Can we finish it this week?
conversation.answer: Maybe, but we need to take QA time into account.

meaningEn: very probable
examples: It is highly likely to rain tonight.
The bug is highly likely related to caching.
It is highly likely that practice will improve your speaking.

conversation.question: Will the client ask for changes?
conversation.answer: Yes, it's highly likely after the demo.

meaningEn: a large or important improvement
examples: There has been a significant improvement in his English.
The new design is a significant improvement.
Daily review can lead to significant improvement over time.

conversation.question: Is the new version better?
conversation.answer: Yes, there is a significant improvement in loading speed.

meaningEn: a small problem
examples: It is just a minor issue.
We found a minor issue in the UI.
A minor issue can become serious if ignored.

conversation.question: Is this bug serious?
conversation.answer: No, it's a minor issue. I can fix it quickly.

meaningEn: an important worry or problem
examples: Security is a major concern.
Performance is a major concern for this page.
Time management is a major concern for many learners.

conversation.question: Can we ignore mobile layout?
conversation.answer: No, responsiveness is a major concern.

meaningEn: to become familiar with something
examples: You'll get used to the new schedule.
It takes time to get used to a large codebase.
I'm getting used to speaking English in meetings.

conversation.question: Is Cursor hard to use?
conversation.answer: A little at first, but you'll get used to it.

meaningEn: to mention a topic
examples: She brought up an interesting point.
I will bring up the API issue in the meeting.
Don't be afraid to bring up your ideas.

conversation.question: Should I mention the delay?
conversation.answer: Yes, bring it up clearly in the meeting.

meaningEn: to divide something into smaller parts or explain simply
meaningVi: chia nhỏ, giải thích từng phần
examples: Let's break down the problem.
The mentor broke down the architecture step by step.
I break down new vocabulary into meaning, form, and examples.

conversation.question: This task feels too big.
conversation.answer: Let's break it down into smaller tickets.

meaningEn: to contact someone again or continue after something
examples: I'll follow up with you tomorrow.
Please follow up with QA after the fix.
A good learner follows up on mistakes.

conversation.question: Did Mike reply?
conversation.answer: Not yet. I'll follow up later today.

meaningEn: to investigate or examine something
examples: I'll look into the problem.
Can you look into the slow page load?
You should look into better ways to practice speaking.

conversation.question: Why is the build failing?
conversation.answer: I'm not sure. I'll look into it now.

meaningEn: to prepare or arrange something for use
examples: I set up my desk every morning.
We need to set up the project first.
She set up a study plan for English.

conversation.question: Is the project ready?
conversation.answer: Almost. I still need to set up Tailwind.

meaningEn: to experience a problem unexpectedly
examples: We ran into a problem during testing.
I ran into a TypeScript error.
Learners often run into pronunciation problems.

conversation.question: Did the deployment succeed?
conversation.answer: Not yet. We ran into a build issue.

meaningEn: to find a temporary way to avoid a problem
examples: We found a workaround for the bug.
The team worked around the API limitation.
Sometimes you need to work around your schedule to keep learning.

conversation.question: Can we fix it today?
conversation.answer: Maybe not, but we can work around it for now.

meaningEn: to find or meet something by chance
examples: I came across a useful article.
I came across this bug while testing.
You may come across many new expressions in meetings.

conversation.question: Where did you find this phrase?
conversation.answer: I came across it in a podcast.

meaningEn: to review, examine, or experience something
examples: Let's go through the document together.
I went through the PR comments this morning.
When learning words, go through examples carefully.

conversation.question: Can you explain the ticket?
conversation.answer: Sure, let's go through it step by step.

meaningEn: to communicate an idea clearly
examples: He got his point across clearly.
The design should get the main message across.
Good examples help you get your ideas across.

conversation.question: Was my explanation clear?
conversation.answer: Yes, you got your point across well.

meaningEn: to create or organize something by combining parts
examples: I put together a list of ideas.
She put together a quick demo.
I put together examples to practice speaking.

conversation.question: Do we have a report?
conversation.answer: Not yet, but I'll put one together.

meaningEn: to start controlling or being responsible for something
examples: She will take over the project next week.
I took over the frontend task after Huy left.
A good handoff helps someone take over smoothly.

conversation.question: Who will handle the task?
conversation.answer: I'll take over from here.

meaningEn: to give responsibility to someone else
examples: I need to hand over my tasks before vacation.
The developer handed over the feature to QA.
Clear notes make it easier to hand over work.

conversation.question: Are you still working on the ticket?
conversation.answer: No, I'll hand it over to QA today.

meaningEn: to record or remember information
examples: I use an app to keep track of expenses.
We need to keep track of user progress.
Keep track of new words you learn every day.

conversation.question: How do you remember vocab?
conversation.answer: I keep track of it in my review app.

meaningEn: to manage something well and not fall behind
examples: She stays on top of her tasks.
We need to stay on top of production bugs.
Daily review helps you stay on top of vocabulary.

conversation.question: Are you behind on the project?
conversation.answer: No, I'm staying on top of it.

meaningEn: to know that something exists or matters
examples: Be aware of your tone when speaking.
Developers should be aware of accessibility issues.
Be aware of common pronunciation mistakes.

conversation.question: Should we worry about mobile users?
conversation.answer: Yes, we should be aware of their experience.

meaningEn: because of something that happened
examples: He practiced daily; as a result, he improved.
The API was slow; as a result, the page loaded late.
I reviewed vocabulary every night; as a result, I remembered more words.

conversation.question: Did the new cache help?
conversation.answer: Yes, as a result, the page feels much faster.

meaningEn: used to show a different or opposite idea
examples: The tool is powerful. On the other hand, it can be expensive.
Remote work is flexible. On the other hand, communication is harder.
AI helps learning. On the other hand, you still need practice.

conversation.question: Should we use a complex solution?
conversation.answer: It is scalable. On the other hand, it may slow us down.

meaningEn: used to introduce your opinion
examples: From my perspective, the plan is realistic.
From my perspective, the UI is too complex.
From my perspective, speaking practice is more useful than memorizing lists.

conversation.question: Do you agree with this approach?
conversation.answer: From my perspective, yes, but we should simplify it.

meaningEn: used to give a strong but thoughtful opinion
examples: I would argue that consistency matters more than intensity.
I would argue that clean architecture saves time later.
I would argue that examples are essential for vocabulary learning.

conversation.question: Is speed the most important thing?
conversation.answer: I would argue that reliability is more important.

meaningEn: used to say the answer changes based on conditions
examples: It depends on your schedule.
It depends on the project requirements.
It depends on how often you review the words.

conversation.question: Should we use Zustand?
conversation.answer: It depends on the complexity of the state.

meaningEn: to be expected or required to do something
examples: You are supposed to submit it today.
The button is supposed to open a modal.
This app is supposed to help you review vocabulary.

conversation.question: Why is this hidden?
conversation.answer: It is supposed to be hidden until the user clicks Show.

meaningEn: used to talk about a specific aspect
examples: In terms of cost, this option is better.
In terms of performance, this component needs improvement.
In terms of speaking, examples matter a lot.

conversation.question: Which tool is better?
conversation.answer: In terms of coding speed, Cursor is stronger.

meaningEn: because of
examples: The flight was delayed due to bad weather.
The page is slow due to large images.
I missed practice due to a busy schedule.

conversation.question: Why did the test fail?
conversation.answer: It failed due to a missing mock.

meaningEn: used to add another idea
examples: The app is useful. In addition, it is easy to use.
We fixed the bug. In addition, we added tests.
In addition, you should practice speaking with examples.

conversation.question: Did you update the UI?
conversation.answer: Yes. In addition, I improved the empty state.

meaningEn: for example
examples: Some habits are useful; for instance, daily review.
Frontend tools change quickly; for instance, React has new patterns.
Use practical phrases; for instance, 'from my perspective'.

conversation.question: Can you give an example?
conversation.answer: For instance, you can use this phrase in meetings.

meaningEn: used to compare differences
examples: This solution is simple. In contrast, the old one was complex.
React is flexible. In contrast, some frameworks are more opinionated.
Memorizing is passive. In contrast, speaking practice is active.

conversation.question: Is this design the same?
conversation.answer: No, in contrast, it is much cleaner.

meaningEn: partly, but not completely
meaningVi: ở một mức độ nào đó
examples: I agree to some extent.
AI can help to some extent, but you need practice.
The issue affects performance to some extent.

conversation.question: Do you think Cursor can replace learning?
conversation.answer: To some extent, it helps, but you still need to understand the code.

meaningEn: used to give an approximate explanation
examples: Roughly speaking, it takes two weeks.
Roughly speaking, the component has three responsibilities.
Roughly speaking, B2 means you can discuss many topics clearly.

conversation.question: How long will this take?
conversation.answer: Roughly speaking, one or two days.

meaningEn: large in amount, value, or importance
examples: They made substantial progress.
The refactor requires substantial effort.
A substantial vocabulary helps you express ideas better.

conversation.question: Is this change small?
conversation.answer: No, it is a substantial change.

meaningEn: simple and easy to understand
examples: The instructions are straightforward.
This component is straightforward to test.
A straightforward example is easier to remember.

conversation.question: Is the setup difficult?
conversation.answer: No, it's pretty straightforward.

meaningEn: correct and exact
examples: The answer is accurate.
We need accurate user data.
Accurate pronunciation helps speaking sound clearer.

conversation.question: Is this translation correct?
conversation.answer: Yes, it looks accurate.

meaningEn: happening in the same way over time
examples: Consistent practice is important.
The UI should be consistent across pages.
Consistent review helps vocabulary stick.

conversation.question: How do I improve speaking?
conversation.answer: Be consistent and practice a little every day.

meaningEn: able to change easily according to needs
examples: My schedule is flexible.
The layout should be flexible on mobile.
A flexible learning plan is easier to maintain.

conversation.question: Can we change the meeting time?
conversation.answer: Yes, I'm flexible this afternoon.

meaningEn: working well without wasting time or energy
examples: This method is efficient.
The new algorithm is more efficient.
Reviewing with flashcards is an efficient way to learn.

conversation.question: Should we automate this?
conversation.answer: Yes, it would be more efficient.

meaningEn: very important or necessary
examples: Sleep is essential for health.
Testing is essential before release.
Examples are essential for speaking practice.

conversation.question: Do we need this feature?
conversation.answer: Yes, it's essential for the MVP.

meaningEn: useful, important, or worth a lot
examples: Your feedback is valuable.
Code review is valuable for learning.
Speaking practice is valuable if you want real fluency.

conversation.question: Was the meeting useful?
conversation.answer: Yes, we got valuable feedback.

meaningEn: difficult but interesting
examples: The task is challenging.
Debugging production issues can be challenging.
Learning collocations is challenging but useful.

conversation.question: Is the project easy?
conversation.answer: Not really, but it is challenging in a good way.

meaningEn: useful in real situations
examples: This is practical advice.
We need a practical solution.
Use practical examples when learning vocabulary.

conversation.question: Should I memorize random words?
conversation.answer: No, focus on practical expressions first.

meaningEn: in a correct or suitable way
examples: Please install it properly.
The form should validate properly.
You need to review words properly, not just read them once.

conversation.question: Is the app working?
conversation.answer: Not properly. The filter is broken.

meaningEn: in the end, after some time
examples: Eventually, you will improve.
The bug was eventually fixed.
If you practice daily, you will eventually speak more naturally.

conversation.question: Did you solve it?
conversation.answer: Yes, eventually I found the root cause.

meaningEn: slowly over a period of time
examples: Your English will gradually improve.
We gradually migrated the old components.

conversation.question: How can I reach B2?
conversation.answer: Gradually build your vocabulary and speaking confidence.

meaningEn: mainly or usually
examples: I mostly work from home.
The app is mostly finished.
I mostly learn vocabulary through examples.

conversation.question: Is the feature done?
conversation.answer: Mostly, but I still need to add tests.

meaningEn: in a way that is easy to understand
examples: Please speak clearly.
The error message should explain the problem clearly.
Try to express your opinion clearly.

conversation.question: Did I explain it well?
conversation.answer: Yes, you explained it clearly.

meaningEn: using few words or for a short time
examples: Can you explain it briefly?
Briefly, the issue is caused by state mismatch.
Briefly review new words every morning.

conversation.question: Can you summarize the ticket?
conversation.answer: Sure, I'll explain it briefly.

meaningEn: not long ago
examples: I recently started using Cursor.
We recently updated the design system.
I recently learned some useful expressions.

conversation.question: Did you change the code?
conversation.answer: Yes, I recently refactored this file.

meaningEn: used when something is likely
examples: It will probably rain.
This bug is probably related to caching.

conversation.question: Is this the cause?
conversation.answer: Probably, but we should check the logs.

meaningEn: at the present time
examples: I'm currently working on a side project.
The feature is currently behind a flag.
I'm currently reviewing B1 vocabulary.

conversation.question: Are you available?
conversation.answer: Not now. I'm currently in a meeting.

meaningEn: more than usual or more than others
examples: I enjoy reading, especially about technology.
This issue matters especially on mobile.

conversation.question: Is this important?
conversation.answer: Yes, especially for new users.

meaningEn: to need work, time, or energy
examples: Learning English requires effort.
Building a clean app requires effort.
Improving speaking requires effort and patience.

conversation.question: Is this skill easy to learn?
conversation.answer: Not exactly. It requires effort.

meaningEn: to improve or move forward
examples: You are making progress.
The team made progress on the feature.
I make progress when I review vocabulary daily.

conversation.question: Am I improving?
conversation.answer: Yes, you are making progress every week.

meaningEn: to become more confident over time
examples: Practice helps build confidence.
Small wins build confidence in coding.
Speaking with examples can build confidence.

conversation.question: I'm nervous in meetings.
conversation.answer: Practice short answers to build confidence.

meaningEn: to communicate thoughts clearly
examples: She can express ideas clearly.
Good vocabulary helps you express ideas at work.
I want to express ideas naturally in English.

conversation.question: Why learn collocations?
conversation.answer: They help you express ideas more naturally.

meaningEn: to achieve something you want
examples: You can reach your goal with practice.
We reached our sprint goal.
To reach a language goal, you need a clear plan.

conversation.question: Can I get to B2 this year?
conversation.answer: Yes, if you practice consistently, you can reach that goal.

meaningEn: to speak more smoothly and naturally
examples: Reading aloud can improve fluency.
Using real examples improves fluency.
I practice daily to improve fluency.

conversation.question: How can I speak better?
conversation.answer: Use common phrases to improve fluency.

meaningEn: a phrase that sounds normal to native speakers
examples: This is a natural expression.
Try to learn natural expressions, not just single words.
Using natural expressions makes your speaking smoother.

conversation.question: Does this sentence sound okay?
conversation.answer: Yes, it's a natural expression.

meaningEn: a mistake many people make
examples: This is a common mistake.
A common mistake is forgetting loading states.
Using only single words is a common mistake in vocabulary learning.

conversation.question: Why does this sound unnatural?
conversation.answer: It's a common mistake with collocations.

meaningEn: an easy-to-understand explanation
examples: Thanks for the clear explanation.
The PR needs a clear explanation.
A clear explanation helps learners remember new words.

conversation.question: Did my answer help?
conversation.answer: Yes, it was a clear explanation.

meaningEn: a firm or confident belief
examples: She has a strong opinion about remote work.
The designer had a strong opinion on spacing.
I have a strong opinion that examples improve speaking.

conversation.question: Do you agree?
conversation.answer: Yes, and I have a strong opinion about this.

meaningEn: a sensible way to do something
examples: That is a reasonable approach.
Using JSON first is a reasonable approach for an MVP.
A reasonable approach is to learn by level and context.

conversation.question: Should we add a backend now?
conversation.answer: No, JSON files are a reasonable approach for the MVP.

meaningEn: an advantage that appears over a long period
examples: Exercise has long-term benefits.
Clean code has long-term benefits.
Learning collocations brings long-term benefits for speaking.

conversation.question: Is daily review worth it?
conversation.answer: Yes, it has a long-term benefit.

meaningEn: a temporary way to solve a problem
examples: This is only a short-term solution.
Hardcoding data is a short-term solution.
Memorizing before a test is a short-term solution.

conversation.question: Should we use local JSON?
conversation.answer: For now, yes. It's a short-term solution for the MVP.

meaningEn: easy for users to understand and use
examples: The app should be user-friendly.
A user-friendly error message helps users recover.
Your vocabulary app should feel user-friendly.

conversation.question: Is the form too complex?
conversation.answer: Yes, we need to make it more user-friendly.

meaningEn: easy to change, fix, and improve over time
examples: The code should be maintainable.
Small components are more maintainable.
A maintainable learning app needs a clear data structure.

conversation.question: Why split the store?
conversation.answer: It makes the code more maintainable.

meaningEn: able to grow without major problems
examples: The system is scalable.
This folder structure is scalable.
Your vocabulary data model should be scalable.

conversation.question: Can this support 10,000 words later?
conversation.answer: Yes, if we design a scalable structure.

meaningEn: easy to read and understand
examples: The text is readable.
Readable code is easier to review.
Readable examples help learners remember words.

conversation.question: Is my code okay?
conversation.answer: Yes, but make it more readable.

meaningEn: able to be used again in different places
examples: This component is reusable.
Reusable hooks reduce duplication.
Reusable example patterns help speaking practice.

conversation.question: Should we create a hook?
conversation.answer: Yes, it will make the logic reusable.

meaningEn: happening in a way that is expected and easy to understand
examples: The behavior should be predictable.
Predictable state management helps debugging.
A predictable review schedule helps memory.

conversation.question: Why use Zustand carefully?
conversation.answer: To keep state predictable.

meaningEn: the state of being complicated
examples: The project has some complexity.
We should avoid unnecessary complexity.
Grammar complexity can make speaking harder.

conversation.question: Why not add backend now?
conversation.answer: It adds complexity too early.

meaningEn: a balance where you gain one thing but lose another
examples: There is always a trade-off.
Using JSON is simple, but the trade-off is limited querying.
Learning many words fast has a trade-off: lower retention.

conversation.question: Should we use local storage?
conversation.answer: Yes, but the trade-off is limited cross-device sync.

meaningEn: an unusual situation that may cause problems
examples: This is an edge case.
We need to handle edge cases in the filter.
A learner may know a word but forget its usage; that's an edge case.

conversation.question: Should we test empty data?
conversation.answer: Yes, it's an important edge case.

meaningEn: the main reason a problem happens
examples: We need to find the root cause.
The root cause was a missing dependency.
The root cause of slow progress may be lack of review.

conversation.question: Why did this fail?
conversation.answer: The root cause is incorrect state update.

meaningEn: extra work created by quick or poor technical decisions
examples: The project has technical debt.
Ignoring tests creates technical debt.
A messy vocabulary schema can become technical debt.

conversation.question: Should we clean this now?
conversation.answer: Yes, otherwise it becomes technical debt.

meaningEn: one reliable place where information is stored
meaningVi: nguồn thông tin chính xác duy nhất
examples: The JSON file is the single source of truth.
The store should not duplicate server data without reason.
A vocabulary item should have one single source of truth.

conversation.question: Where should the level data live?
conversation.answer: The vocabulary JSON should be the single source of truth.

meaningEn: keeping different responsibilities in separate parts of code
examples: Good architecture uses separation of concerns.
UI components should not contain too much business logic.
Separation of concerns makes the app easier to extend.

conversation.question: Why create a repository layer?
conversation.answer: For better separation of concerns.

meaningEn: something that makes people confused
examples: The old label was a source of confusion.
Inconsistent naming is a source of confusion.
Similar phrases can be a source of confusion for learners.

conversation.question: Why do users click the wrong button?
conversation.answer: The button label is a source of confusion.

meaningEn: the time and effort needed to learn something new
meaningVi: độ khó ban đầu khi học
examples: Cursor has a learning curve.
TypeScript has a learning curve for beginners.
Collocations have a learning curve, but they are worth it.

conversation.question: Is Zustand hard?
conversation.answer: Not too hard, but there is a small learning curve.

meaningEn: a recommended and effective way to do something
examples: This is a common best practice.
Writing tests is a best practice.
Reviewing vocabulary with examples is a best practice.

conversation.question: Should we validate JSON data?
conversation.answer: Yes, that's a best practice.

meaningEn: a simple practical rule that is usually useful
examples: As a rule of thumb, keep components small.
A good rule of thumb is to review new words within 24 hours.
As a rule of thumb, avoid premature optimization.

conversation.question: How often should I review?
conversation.answer: As a rule of thumb, review difficult words more often.

meaningEn: improving performance too early before it is needed
examples: Avoid premature optimization.
Adding caching now may be premature optimization.
Trying to memorize C2 words first can be premature optimization.

conversation.question: Should we add virtualization now?
conversation.answer: No, that may be premature optimization.

meaningEn: to do more than expected
meaningVi: nỗ lực hơn mức yêu cầu
examples: She always goes the extra mile.
The developer went the extra mile and added tests.
If you go the extra mile, your English will improve faster.

conversation.question: Why is she trusted?
conversation.answer: Because she always goes the extra mile.

meaningEn: to set a higher standard
examples: This project raises the bar.
Good documentation raises the bar for the team.
Practicing with real conversations raises the bar for vocabulary learning.

conversation.question: Why add conversations to each word?
conversation.answer: It raises the bar for practical learning.

meaningEn: to do something badly or cheaply to save time or money
meaningVi: làm qua loa, cắt giảm chất lượng
examples: Don't cut corners on safety.
We shouldn't cut corners with testing.
If you cut corners while learning, you may forget quickly.

conversation.question: Can we skip validation?
conversation.answer: No, we shouldn't cut corners.

meaningEn: to make progress or continue
examples: Let's move forward with the plan.
We can move forward after QA approves it.
To improve English, keep moving forward even slowly.

conversation.question: Should we wait?
conversation.answer: No, let's move forward with the MVP.

meaningEn: to make less progress than expected
examples: Don't fall behind in class.
The project fell behind schedule.
If you stop reviewing, you may fall behind.

conversation.question: Are we on track?
conversation.answer: Not really. We're starting to fall behind.

meaningEn: to reach the same level after being behind
examples: I need to catch up on lessons.
We need to catch up with the sprint plan.
I missed practice yesterday, so I need to catch up today.

conversation.question: Did you finish the tasks?
conversation.answer: Not yet, but I'll catch up tonight.

meaningEn: to be easily noticed because of being special or better
examples: Her portfolio stands out.
Clean UI can make your app stand out.
Using natural expressions helps your speaking stand out.

conversation.question: How can I improve my resume?
conversation.answer: Build projects that stand out.

meaningEn: to take action or improve effort when needed
examples: He stepped up during the crisis.
The team stepped up before the deadline.

conversation.question: Who will handle the urgent bug?
conversation.answer: I'll step up and take it.
